title: Iacob and Iosep
summary: The repository XML is a local copy or version of University of Michigan CME item ACB1675.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR/TEI-in-Libraries Level 1 encoding of Arthur S. Napier's 1916 Clarendon Press edition. Napier independently transcribed the secure witness, Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 652, fols. 1r–10v, while consulting Wilhelm Heuser's distinct 1905 edition; Napier's volume also contains two selected manuscript facsimiles. U-M links 78 page images of the whole printed volume but OCR-encodes chiefly the title page and 538-line poem, omitting the introduction, notes, and glossary as text. Separate public reproductions include an Internet Archive scan of Napier, a one-canvas Digital Bodleian image of fol. 1r, and a complete Yale facsimile of Takamiya MS 32. The faded text at Takamiya fol. 3r is only a candidate related Jacob-and-Joseph fragment, not a confirmed second witness.
review_notes: The cataloged work is the anonymous Middle English poem, not Bodley 652 as a composite codex, Napier's edition, U-M's OCR, or any digital facsimile.

MS. Bodl. 652 is the current shelfmark; Summary Catalogue 2306 is retained as a historical catalog designation. Yale's current item record gives Takamiya MS 32 1, while scholarship and the collection guide use Takamiya MS 32.
The one-canvas Digital Bodleian object is a selected image of fol. 1r, not a complete facsimile. Napier's two plates likewise reproduce only fols. 8v and 9r.
ARLIMA reports a 1998 reprint of Napier, which is modeled separately because no evidence shows that U-M encoded or scanned that reprint.
Current U-M No Copyright–United States language and the legacy XML's generic copyright/restriction notice are both preserved at their own layers and dates.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for acb1675
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item ACB1675.0001.001
entities.description: A publicly viewable, item-specific uncorrected OCR encoding of Napier's title page, Middle English poem, and reading notes. The header says no OCR correction or content editing was performed, identifies TEI in Libraries Level 1, and links the text to page images.
notes: The inherited bibliographic AUTHOR field names Napier, but he is editor of an anonymous medieval work.
The encoding contains 538 L, 20 manuscript-folio MILESTONE, 15 NOTE1, 78 PB, and no LG elements.
Most of Napier's introduction (pp. vii–xxxii), notes (pp. 19–23), and glossary (pp. 24–41) are represented only by PB links inside divisions typed as omitted front or back matter.

entities.label: University of Michigan page images for ACB1675.0001.001
entities.description: The 78 page images linked from U-M's encoding, covering the printed volume from preliminary leaves through page 41 and terminal blanks, including the in-volume double facsimile.
entities.reproduction_format: 78 600 dpi TIFF G4 page images delivered through linked page views
notes: Public page viewing was verified; anonymous bulk retrieval of the source TIFF set was not verified.

entities.label: Napier 1916 edition of Iacob and Iosep

bibliographic.citation: Iacob and Iosep: A Middle English Poem of the Thirteenth Century, ed. Arthur S. Napier (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1916).
bibliographic.title: Iacob and Iosep: A Middle English Poem of the Thirteenth Century

notes: Napier states that he copied the poem years before Heuser's 1905 edition appeared, then resumed his independent edition because the earlier publication was difficult to access.

The volume contains an English introduction (pp. vii–xxxii), the Middle English text with footnote readings and folio locators (pp. 1–18), notes (pp. 19–23), glossary (pp. 24–41), and two facsimile plates.

Napier supplies the bracketed opening heading, marks direct speech with modern punctuation, prints the manuscript explicit, discusses but rejects uncertain emendations proposed by Heuser, and records the leaf cut out between fols. 6 and 7.

entities.label: Napier's double facsimile of Bodley 652 fols. 8v and 9r
entities.description: Two monochrome plates bound into the 1916 edition, reproducing the consecutive manuscript pages carrying approximately lines 406–459.

entities.label: Heuser 1905 edition of Das frühmittelenglische Josephlied

bibliographic.citation: Wilhelm Heuser, ‘Das frühmittelenglische Josephlied (Ms. Bodl. 652),’ Bonner Beiträge zur Anglistik 17 (1905): 83–121.
bibliographic.title: Das frühmittelenglische Josephlied (Ms. Bodl. 652)

notes: This is a distinct prior edition. Napier consulted it but states that his own transcription predated it, so Heuser is not modeled as the text from which Napier copied.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Bonner Beiträge zur Anglistik 17
entities.description: A complete scan of the 1905 journal volume whose pp. 83–121 contain Heuser's edition of Iacob and Iosep. The digital object is broader than the cataloged article.
entities.reproduction_format: 4,762,223-byte Image Container PDF with DjVu and OCR derivatives

entities.label: Reported 1998 reprint of Napier's Iacob and Iosep

bibliographic.citation: Arthur S. Napier, ed., Iacob and Iosep: A Middle English Poem of the Thirteenth Century (reported reprint, 1998; publisher and issue details not verified).
bibliographic.title: Iacob and Iosep: A Middle English Poem of the Thirteenth Century

notes: The reprint is not merged with the 1916 issue and is not placed upstream of U-M because no inspected source identifies U-M's copy as the 1998 reprint.

entities.label: Internet Archive Google scan of Napier's 1916 edition
entities.description: A 90-page, 600 ppi scan digitized by Google and uploaded to Internet Archive through ARLIMA in 2022; the downloadable PDF was retrieved and inspected.
entities.reproduction_format: 90-page, 3,758,460-byte PDF with OCR derivatives; 600 ppi source metadata
notes: The downloaded PDF has SHA-256 85efb8c67788baaa0e995d8d66573b999b089bf806edc4166a482f26b539424b.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 652, fols. 1r–10v
entities.description: The secure manuscript witness of Iacob and Iosep in the first textual component of a composite parchment codex. The poem occupies fols. 1r–10v, and one leaf has been cut out between fols. 6 and 7.

date_statements.label: Napier's dating of the poem hand
date_statements.value: soon after the middle of the thirteenth century
date_statements.notes: Napier distinguishes this first part's hand from the three-part codex as a whole; the difference from the current institutional end-of-century label remains unresolved.

holding.notes: The repository-owned catalog record is used here; scholarly handlists such as ARLIMA and PCMEP remain separate evidence.

physical_description.extent: Iacob and Iosep at fols. 1r–10v; one leaf missing between fols. 6 and 7
notes: Digital Bodleian identifies the object as a composite manuscript from Southwest England and describes the selected fol. 1r as a text page with a decorated initial and red penwork flourishing.
Napier describes three separately written codicological parts, all from second-half thirteenth-century England, and assigns the poem to the first part.
Adjacent codex contents include French Lucidarium, Letter of Prester John, and Chasteau d'Amour texts plus Latin motets; they are not parts of Iacob and Iosep.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian image of MS. Bodl. 652 fol. 1r
entities.description: A one-canvas digital object derived from 35mm film, showing the opening manuscript page of Iacob and Iosep; it is not complete manuscript coverage.
entities.reproduction_format: one 3031 × 4637 pixel IIIF image derived from 35mm slide or filmstrip

entities.label: Iacob and Iosep witness tradition
entities.description: An aggregate research description separating the secure Bodley 652 text from the candidate Takamiya MS 32 fol. 3r fragment. It is not itself a physical manuscript and does not count the candidate as confirmed.

entities.label: Beinecke Takamiya MS 32, fol. 3r (candidate Iacob and Iosep fragment)
entities.description: A fifteenth-century composite literary manuscript whose faded or reused fol. 3r has been proposed as preserving a related Jacob-and-Joseph fragment. Yale's catalog does not identify that folio as Iacob and Iosep, and the exact textual relationship remains tentative.

date_statements.label: production of the codex
date_statements.notes: This dates the codex as cataloged, not an independently dated Jacob-and-Joseph fragment.

holding.notes: Scholarship commonly shortens the call number to Takamiya MS 32; the reviewed Yale item manifest gives Takamiya MS 32 1.

notes: Yale catalogs the codex as English, circa 1425–1450, in one hand, generally two columns of 39–44 lines, and bound in nineteenth-century red morocco.
Its cataloged contents include the Canterbury Tales, Gower extracts, and several Middle English poems, including a distinct Vision of Tundale; the catalog does not list Iacob and Iosep.
Visual inspection of the public fol. 3r canvas confirms a difficult faded or reused text page but cannot by itself establish its relation to Bodley 652.

entities.label: Yale complete digital facsimile of Takamiya MS 32
entities.description: A complete 366-canvas IIIF facsimile with a public PDF rendering; fol. 3r is publicly viewable within it.
entities.reproduction_format: 366-canvas IIIF Presentation 3 object with JPEG images and full PDF rendering

agents.name: ARLIMA — Archives de littérature du Moyen Âge
Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

scope.notes: Identifiers, header, OCR text, and image references match; the exact transfer date is undocumented.

object.description: Title page, poem pp. 1–18, footnote readings, and page-level structure; most introduction, notes, and glossary omitted as OCR text
The complete printed volume as scanned, including blanks and facsimile plates

subject.description: Edited Middle English text on pp. 1–18

scope.notes: Napier directly copied the manuscript and presents readings/emendations in notes; the text is not a facsimile.

subject.description: Introduction, metrical analysis, and selected textual decisions

scope.notes: Napier acknowledges and discusses Heuser but says his own manuscript copy was made before Heuser appeared.

subject.description: Scanned journal pages containing Heuser's article and edition

scope.notes: The digital object scans the entire volume, so only its pp. 83–121 reproduce this edition entity.

subject.description: Two unnumbered facsimile plates between printed pp. 8 and 9

object.description: A physical copy of the 1916 Clarendon edition

scope.notes: No evidence was found for revision, reset, publisher, or issue details; the relation records only ARLIMA's explicit reprint report.

subject.description: The secure complete surviving witness, though internally lacunose
A proposed but unresolved related fragment

object.description: Faded or reused text proposed as related

scope.notes: The cataloged manuscript as a whole is not asserted to be a witness; only the proposed fol. 3r text is in question.

assertion.notes: A qualified scholarly proposal requiring transcription and collation.

object.description: The physical Delamere Chaucer codex, including candidate fol. 3r

access.access_method: Read the checked-out CME submodule path; the same repository is public on GitHub.
Open the exact U-M full-text division or persistent handle in a browser.
Use page-image links from the U-M item/full-text viewer.
access.format: web page images linked from 78 PB milestones
notes: Page viewing is public, but the original 600 dpi TIFF set was not verified as an anonymous bulk download.

access.access_method: Open the Internet Archive book reader.
Download the verified Text PDF directly.
Download the scanned Bonner Beiträge volume containing Heuser's article.
access.format: 4,762,223-byte Image Container PDF; Heuser at pp. 83–121

access.access_method: Consult ARLIMA's public year-only bibliographic report. Targeted title/year/catalog searches did not identify a verifiable publisher record or public copy; this access record therefore documents catalog evidence, not a repository-held deliverable.
Open the exact repository-owned medieval-manuscripts catalog record.
Open the one-canvas Digital Bodleian object or IIIF manifest.
notes: This access record covers only fol. 1r and must not be described as complete manuscript digitization.

access.access_method: Register for a Bodleian Reader card and order the manuscript for consultation in the Weston Library Special Collections reading room, subject to handling approval.
Request reference scans or higher-resolution imaging through Bodleian Special Collections copying services; fulfillment depends on condition, rights, and service review.
access.format: mediated reference scans or commissioned imaging

access.access_method: Open the Yale Digital Collections item or IIIF manifest and navigate to fol. 3r.
Download the full PDF rendering exposed in the IIIF manifest.
Register and request the item in advance for the Beinecke reading room; visiting researchers present photo identification and requests normally require lead time.

rights.component: local XML transcription, markup, and incorporated edition content
rights.basis: The artifact is a local copy or version of the U-M item currently labeled No Copyright–United States. Its embedded 2006 availability notice remains generic and conditional, so repository availability or licensing does not independently settle every incorporated component.
notes: The public git repository does not override the U-M source's legacy notice, jurisdiction limit, or third-party image terms.

rights.component: U-M digital text and item-level presentation
rights.basis: The current U-M item labels the materials public domain and applies RightsStatements.org No Copyright–United States; that statement records provider belief and is not a license or worldwide determination.
notes: Do not propagate this US-qualified statement automatically to Bodleian or Yale manuscript images.

rights.basis: The embedded XML says, ‘Where applicable, subject to copyright’ and warns that other distribution restrictions may apply without identifying a component, jurisdiction, or license.
notes: Preserved separately from the more specific current NoC-US item statement rather than silently overwritten.

rights.component: 78 U-M page images of the 1916 volume
rights.basis: The current item-level No Copyright–United States statement accompanies the page-image-bearing item, while exact source-image ownership and worldwide status are not separately stated.

rights.component: Internet Archive digital scan and its provider-marked source item
rights.basis: Internet Archive metadata applies the Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0. The mark communicates a no-known-copyright assessment rather than a license or warranty.
The current U-M item calls the materials public domain in the United States, and Internet Archive marks its 1916 scan with the Public Domain Mark.
notes: This record does not claim worldwide status for every modern reproduction or interface asset.

rights.component: two manuscript facsimile plates first published in Napier's 1916 volume, excluding later scan files and interfaces
rights.basis: The plates were published in the 1916 volume. U-M currently applies No Copyright–United States to the image-bearing item, Internet Archive applies the Public Domain Mark to its scan, and Copyright Office duration guidance describes the maximum term for pre-1978 published copyright as 95 years.
notes: The historical plates' U.S. status is separate from Digital Bodleian's modern fol. 1r photograph and from provider terms governing later scans.

rights.component: underlying thirteenth-century Iacob and Iosep text at fols. 1r–10v, excluding the physical object and all photographs
rights.basis: The anonymous medieval text was created centuries before 1978 and was published in Heuser's 1905 and Napier's 1916 editions. Current U-M and Internet Archive provider statements treat the published material as free of U.S. copyright restrictions; Copyright Office guidance supplies the relevant finite duration framework for pre-1978 publications.
notes: Ownership and handling rules for the physical manuscript, and copyright or contract terms for modern photographs, remain separate layers.

rights.component: Heuser's 1905 article, edited text, and original scholarly apparatus, excluding later scan files and interfaces
rights.basis: The article and edition were first published in 1905. U.S. Copyright Office duration guidance gives eligible pre-1978 published copyrights a maximum term of 95 years, which had expired before this review.
notes: This U.S. content-status record does not determine copyright or contract terms for Internet Archive's later scan derivative, which remains separately unknown.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan of the 1905 Bonner Beiträge volume
rights.basis: The exact Internet Archive item exposes a public scan but its reviewed metadata supplies no item-specific license URL; public access alone is not a reuse license.

rights.component: reported 1998 reprint as an unidentified material issue
rights.basis: Only the year and reprint relationship were found; publisher, new matter, jurisdiction, and rights statement remain unidentified.

rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and reuse under provider terms
rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest attributes the photograph © Bodleian Libraries and assigns CC BY-NC 4.0; Digital Bodleian terms require attribution and restrict licensed reuse to noncommercial purposes.
notes: This exact image license does not automatically govern newly commissioned Bodleian scans or Napier's historical plates.

rights.component: Yale digital images and PDF of Takamiya MS 32
rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest has no rights field and only a provider statement. Yale and Beinecke general pages place permission analysis on the user and request citation; they do not supply an item-specific open license for this facsimile.

rights.component: underlying medieval manuscript text, excluding modern photography and interface assets
rights.basis: Beinecke states that public-domain collection material does not require library permission and that it generally does not hold copyright, but the exact item lacks a specific rights field; image and contractual layers remain separate.

editorial_practices.source_feature: One Middle English work occupying the first ten folios of a composite multilingual codex
editorial_practices.representation: The complete extant poem is edited as a standalone volume; adjacent French and Latin works are described but not transcribed.
editorial_practices.description: Napier isolates Bodley 652 fols. 1r–10v as the textual object of the edition and records the missing leaf between fols. 6 and 7.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The manuscript has no opening heading but ends with ‘Explicit Iacob & Iosep.’
editorial_practices.representation: Napier prints [IACOB AND IOSEP] in brackets and footnotes ‘No heading in MS.’; the explicit is retained at the end.
editorial_practices.description: The opening title is visibly supplied rather than presented as manuscript rubrication.
editorial_practices.scope: heading and explicit of the edited poem

editorial_practices.source_feature: Middle English verse with extensive direct speech
editorial_practices.representation: Modern punctuation and quotation marks distinguish speakers and sentences; footnotes preserve selected manuscript readings and proposed corrections.
editorial_practices.description: Napier's punctuation is editorial and should not be treated as a facsimile of manuscript pointing.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Napier's independent manuscript copy and Heuser's 1905 prior edition
editorial_practices.representation: Napier cites Heuser's observations and emendations, accepting some analysis while rejecting changes he considers uncertain.
editorial_practices.description: The prior edition informs discussion and selected decisions but is not the source of Napier's transcription.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Napier's full volume with introduction, poem, notes, glossary, and two plates
editorial_practices.representation: Uncorrected OCR encodes the title page and poem with footnotes; most front matter and all notes/glossary pages are retained only as PB-linked omitted matter. All 78 page images remain linked.
editorial_practices.description: The digital text is not a full-text transcription of every page in the source volume even though page-image coverage is broader.
notes: The item-specific header explicitly says OCR is uncorrected, no content editing was performed, markup follows TEI in Libraries Level 1, and digital page images are linked.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/acb1675.xml.
evidence.locator: HEADER; FRONT; BODY division ACB1675.0001.001:3; BACK; NOTE1, MILESTONE, PB, and TRAILER elements
evidence.summary: Direct local evidence for the exact artifact, U-M identifiers and date, 1916 source citation, uncorrected-OCR declaration, Level 1 encoding, page-image links, text and omission boundaries, 538 encoded lines, folio references, reading notes, missing leaf, and legacy availability statement.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME public GitHub repository.
evidence.summary: Confirms the public upstream repository route for the submodule that supplies the XML artifact; repository licensing does not override provider rights in incorporated source texts or images.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item ACB1675.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Confirms current public full-text and page-view access, persistent identifier, Napier 1916 source, educational/research presentation, current public-domain wording, and No Copyright–United States item status.
notes: The persistent handle may redirect or reject automated clients while the exact full-text URL remains publicly viewable in a browser.

evidence.citation: RightsStatements.org, No Copyright–United States 1.0.
evidence.summary: Defines NoC-US as a provider belief limited to United States copyright, not a license or warranty, and notes that contractual restrictions may still apply.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.summary: Authoritative guidance on the duration framework for works created or published before 1978, including the 95-year maximum term for eligible pre-1978 published copyrights and the separate treatment of older unpublished works.

evidence.citation: Arthur S. Napier, ed., Iacob and Iosep: A Middle English Poem of the Thirteenth Century (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1916), Internet Archive PDF.
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. vii–xxxii; text pp. 1–18; notes pp. 19–23; glossary pp. 24–41
evidence.summary: Direct source for publication facts, Napier's independent transcription and Heuser consultation, manuscript identity and codicological description, missing leaf, work date/dialect/meter arguments, editorial readings, text, apparatus, and facsimile plates.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of the downloaded Internet Archive PDF of Napier's 1916 edition.
evidence.locator: title and contents pages; text opening; unnumbered double-facsimile leaves; notes and glossary
evidence.summary: Confirms 90 scan pages, edition layout and contents sequence, bracketed supplied heading, poem text with footnotes, blank leaves surrounding two manuscript plates, and the plates' identity as Bodley 652 fols. 8v and 9r rather than a complete manuscript facsimile.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for iacobandiosepedarthurnapier.
evidence.summary: Identifies the 1916 edition, Google digitization, ARLIMA upload on 2022-12-07, 600 ppi source, 90-page PDF, file size and checksums, and Public Domain Mark 1.0.

evidence.citation: Bonner Beiträge zur Anglistik 17 (1905), Internet Archive scan, including Wilhelm Heuser, ‘Das frühmittelenglische Josephlied,’ pp. 83–121.
evidence.summary: Confirms Heuser's distinct 1905 German article and edition in the scanned volume and provides public page/PDF access; the item metadata lacks a reviewed item-specific license URL.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA, ‘Iacob and Iosep.’
evidence.summary: Lists the manuscript explicit, incipit, 538-line extent, Bodley 652 fols. 1r–10v, Heuser's 1905 edition, Napier's 1916 edition, its current digital copies, and an explicit report of a 1998 reprint.

evidence.citation: Targeted web and catalog-style searches for the reported 1998 reprint of Napier's Iacob and Iosep.
evidence.summary: ARLIMA's year-only report was recoverable, but searches for the title, editor, year, publisher, ISBN, and WorldCat-style records did not identify reliable issue details or a public copy as of the review date.
notes: A negative public search is date-bounded and may be overturned by a library catalog or physical title-page inspection.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Bodl. 652.
evidence.summary: Exact repository-owned catalog route for the current shelfmark and physical manuscript. It anchors the holding record separately from scholarly handlists.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian IIIF manifest for Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 652.
evidence.summary: Confirms current shelfmark, title Jacob and Joseph, Middle English, end-thirteenth-century and Southwest England labels, parchment/composite status, exact catalog link, one-canvas fol. 1r coverage from 35mm, image dimensions, Bodleian attribution, and CC BY-NC 4.0 terms.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of the Digital Bodleian IIIF image for MS. Bodl. 652 fol. 1r.
evidence.summary: Confirms that the selected public image shows the poem's opening text page with a decorated initial and one principal text column; it supplies no evidence for complete digital coverage of the codex.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA, manuscript description for Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Bodl. 652.
evidence.summary: Provides a scholarly codex-content handlist, second-half thirteenth-century England context, and the poem at fols. 1r–10v. Its generic layout and digitization statements are not substituted for the current institutional record and IIIF manifest.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, Terms of Use.
evidence.summary: Explains Digital Bodleian image licensing, attribution, noncommercial CC terms where assigned, and the route for permission beyond those terms; used with the exact manifest's CC BY-NC attribution.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Requests and ordering for Special Collections.
evidence.summary: Describes reader registration and advance ordering for manuscript consultation in the Weston Library Special Collections reading rooms.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Special Collections copying and remote access.
evidence.summary: Documents mediated reference-scan and high-resolution imaging routes, possible fees, and condition/legal review; it does not promise complete images or redistribution permission.

evidence.citation: Parsed Corpus of Middle English Poetry, ‘Joseph and Jacob’ text details.
evidence.summary: Confirms the Napier edition, Bodley 652 fols. 1r–10v, title variants, DIMEV/NIMEV identifiers, tentative composition range and dialect qualifications, and editorial treatment of line breaks, quotation marks, and the Latin explicit.
notes: Its displayed 583 line count conflicts with the 538 lines in Napier, Hume, ARLIMA, and direct XML inspection.

evidence.citation: Brian Murdoch, ‘The Middle English Iacob and Iosep and the Medieval Popular Bible,’ English Studies 98.8 (2017): 846–865.
evidence.summary: Analyzes the poem's popular-biblical and French narrative relationships, its complex relation to Cursor Mundi, Jacob's centrality, and possible audience; reports a possibly related fragment in a manuscript then in Tokyo without treating it as secure.

evidence.citation: Cathy Hume, ‘Iacob and Iosep: a happy tale of a knightly family,’ in Middle English Biblical Poetry: Romance, Audience and Tradition (Boydell & Brewer, 2021), pp. 31–58.
evidence.summary: Supplies current scholarly evidence for probable second-half thirteenth-century composition, 538 extant lines, unique Bodley witness and missing leaf, mixed rhyming-couplet meters, romance/biblical contexts, and an explicitly argued rather than certain audience hypothesis.

evidence.citation: Cathy Hume, ‘The Middle English Jacob and Joseph fragment in Takamiya MS 32,’ conference paper, 2021.
evidence.summary: Official institutional record establishes that Hume presented a paper specifically identifying and investigating a Jacob and Joseph fragment in Takamiya MS 32; the page supplies no abstract, transcription, or published conclusion, so exact relationship remains unresolved.

evidence.citation: ‘Old Testament Translation: Eighth to Fourteenth Centuries,’ Oxford Handbook of Medieval Literature in English.
evidence.summary: Describes the anonymous 538-line work as a Genesis 37–47 retelling that blends Vulgate narrative with romance and ballad traditions and introduces vernacular changes.

evidence.citation: Yale University Library IIIF manifest for catalog item 14705200, Canterbury Tales / Delamere Chaucer.
evidence.summary: Confirms the exact Yale item, alternative title, current call number Takamiya MS 32 1, circa 1425–1450 England production, physical description and cataloged contents, complete 366-canvas digitization, public PDF rendering, and absence of an item-level IIIF rights field.

evidence.citation: Beinecke Library, Medieval Manuscripts from the Toshiyuki Takamiya collection.
evidence.summary: Confirms Yale's 2017 acquisition, the scholarly short shelfmark Takamiya MS 32, Delamere Chaucer identity, second-quarter fifteenth-century dating, broad contents, and availability through the Beinecke reading room.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of Yale Digital Collections image 14715830 for Takamiya MS 32 fol. 3r.
evidence.summary: Confirms that the proposed folio is publicly viewable and contains difficult faded or reused writing. Visual inspection alone does not securely identify a passage or prove descent from the Bodley text.

evidence.citation: Beinecke Library, How to Request Items.
evidence.summary: Provides the registration, identification, advance-request, and reading-room route for Yale and visiting researchers.

evidence.citation: Yale Library, Reuse of Library Materials.
evidence.summary: States that users must determine permissions and that Yale makes no rights warranty; general teaching/research policies do not create an item-specific image license.

evidence.citation: Beinecke Library, Copyright Questions.
evidence.summary: Explains that Beinecke generally does not hold copyright, public-domain collection materials do not require library permission, citation is requested, and users remain responsible for legal analysis.

evidence.citation: Beinecke Library, Terms of Use.
evidence.summary: Supplies provider terms for access and scholarly use; it does not fill the exact IIIF manifest's missing item-level rights field with an open license.

open_questions.question: Does Takamiya MS 32 fol. 3r preserve Iacob and Iosep, a reworked descendant, a cognate Jacob-and-Joseph text, or unrelated material?
next_steps: Obtain Hume's 2021 conference paper or subsequent publication and a specialist diplomatic transcription.
Request enhanced or multispectral imaging if ordinary color images remain illegible, then collate the folio against Bodley 652 and both early editions.
Keep the candidate relation tentative and outside the secure witness count until the textual identity is demonstrated.

open_questions.question: How should Napier's ‘soon after the middle’ dating and South-West dialect argument for the poem portion be reconciled with Digital Bodleian's end-thirteenth-century and Southwest England labels and more cautious modern original-dialect assessments?
next_steps: Consult the full current Bodleian catalog description, LAEME and LALME profiles, and recent palaeographic work on the first codicological part.
Keep witness production, scribal dialect, and work composition locality as separately attributed claims.

open_questions.question: Can a complete, current institutional image set of Bodley 652 fols. 1r–10v be obtained, and under what item-specific terms?
next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections imaging services for reference or high-resolution scans of fols. 1r–10v.
Record the supplied files and permission terms as a new reproduction entity rather than extending the one-canvas CC BY-NC record.

open_questions.question: What exact source copy and scanning provenance underlie U-M's 78 page images, and how should the legacy generic restriction notice be reconciled with the current NoC-US item statement for redistribution outside the United States?
next_steps: Request item-level provenance and rights clarification from U-M Library.
Treat the current NoC-US label as jurisdiction-specific and preserve the legacy notice until the provider resolves the scope.

open_questions.question: Which publisher and physical issue does ARLIMA's reported 1998 reprint of Napier's edition refer to, and did it contain any reset, revision, or new matter?
next_steps: Search union catalogs by title/editor, inspect a physical copy, and compare its title and copyright pages, type setting, pagination, preliminaries, plates, and colophon against the 1916 issue.
Retain it as a separate qualified version and do not make it U-M's source without copy-specific evidence.
